It has been demonstrated in section 4.1 that $\ell|F=f$ are identical regardless of $f$ . We thus assume the following condition throughout the derivations.  

This leads to the distributional equivalence of $\hat{p}_{F}\!:\forall\hat{f}^{(i)},\hat{f}^{(j)}\in\hat{\mathcal{F}},P(\ell|F=\hat{f}^{(i)})=P(\ell|F=\hat{f}^{(j)}).$  

4.3 THE DISTRIBUTION OF DATA  

According to the distributional equivalence across models, we note that the point-wise difference can be mitigated when we consider the entire data distribution $p_{X}$ . All the models have the same expected predictions over the entire dataset. Based on this, we then prove the following theorem that explains the improvement of ensembles.  

Theorem 4.1. (Guaranteed Improvement) Given a convex metric $\phi$ , we have:  

$$
\mathbb{E}_{X\sim p x}\left[\phi\Big(\mathbb{E}_{F\sim\hat{p}_{F}}\big[F(X)\big]\Big)\right]\leq\operatorname*{min}_{\hat{f}\in\hat{\mathcal{F}}}\left\{\mathbb{E}_{X\sim p x}\left[\phi\big(\hat{f}(X)\big)\right]\right\}
$$  

![](images/8e225faa4f3ad639df24d1aa7621a1f967868b572aa30524a7c1d45b0098a86a.jpg)  
Figure 4: The estimation (regions) and the empirical results (solid curves) of the Brier score of original models. The $x$ -axis represents the number of ensemble members (a.k.a. ensemble capacity), and the $y$ -axis represents the Brier score of the ensemble. Dashed lines represent the standard deviations. The tested model is CNN.  

3. This shows the condition eq. (4) where deep ensembles are preferred under neural collapse. It demonstrates that deep ensembles always outperform any individual ensemble members (instead of the average loss).  

Although it has been proved above that deep ensembles are guaranteed to outperform any single member, the extent to which the improvement is and what factors contribute to the improvement remains unknown. Therefore, we delve deep into the quantification analysis of the improvement.  

Instantiating $\phi$ to the Brier score $\phi_{B}$ , we have the following theorem to bound the performance shift. Theorem 4.2. (The Brier Score Improvement) The ensemble performance improvement of the Brier score is determined by:  

$$
\Delta_{B}=\operatorname*{min}_{\hat{f}\in\hat{\mathcal{F}}}\mathbb{E}_{X\sim p_{X}}\left[\phi_{B}\big(\hat{f}(X)\big)\right]-\mathbb{E}_{X\sim p_{X}}\left[\phi_{B}\big(\mathbb{E}_{F\sim\hat{p}_{F}}[F(X)]\big)\right]=\rho-\rho^{2}-\operatorname{Var}_{p_{X}}[\hat{f}(X)]
$$  

where $\rho=\mathbb{E}_{X\sim p_{X}}[\hat{f}(X)]\,f o r\,\forall\hat{f}\in\hat{\mathcal{F}}$ . $\Delta_{B}=0$ holds if and only if point-wise equivalence holds.   
$\Delta_{B}=\rho-\rho^{2}$ holds if and only if the ensemble $\bar{\hat{f}}$ predicts constantly for all samples.  

5. The result shows that when the expected prediction of $\hat{p}_{F}$ is determined (as $\dot{\rho_{.}}$ ), then the global diversity $\operatorname{Var}_{X\sim p_{X}}[{\bar{\hat{f}}}(X)]$ is responsible for the improvement. To our best knowledge, previous work has been focusing on point-wise diversity (i.e. variance over $p_{F})$ while the importance of the global diversity (i.e. variance over $p_{X}$ ) has not been recognized before. Now it suffices to determine how the global diversity changes regarding the joint distribution $p_{X}\otimes p_{F}$ . Recall from eq. (7) that if individual models tend to agree more on each sample, the point-wise predictions become closer to either 0 or 1. This will lead to a higher global diversity since the ensembled predictions are deviated from the center. Such agreement can be measured by the probability measure of the shared correct prediction between two models.6:  

Theorem 4.3. Let $F^{-1}(1)\,=\,\{{\pmb x}\,\in\,\chi|F({\pmb x})\,=\,1\}$ denote the subset of $\mathcal{X}$ that $F$ can predict correctly, then $\mathrm{Var}_{X\sim p_{X}}[\hat{f}(X)]=\mathbb{E}_{F_{1},F_{2}\sim\hat{\mathcal{F}}}\big[p_{X}(F_{1}^{-1}(1)\cap F_{2}^{-1}(1))\big]-\rho^{2}$  

This quantifies that the global diversity of $\hat{p}_{F}$ decreases with $\rho$ and increases with the expected agreement across models. We emphasize that this is not counterintuitive since the globalness is regarding $p_{X}$ instead of $p_{F}$ . One important significance of theorem 4.3 is that due to the distributional equivalence property, trained models $F\sim p_{F}$ demonstrate great symmetry, leading to very small variance in $\dot{p_{X}}(\dot{F}_{1}^{-1}\dot{(1)}\cap F_{2}^{-1}(1))$ . Thus it can be estimated by only two models with small errors. As a result, we can foresee how the ensemble will perform with only two models. Besides, due to the associative and commutative nature of multiplication, theorem 4.3 can be migrated to the original function space $\mathcal{F}$ .7:  

Theorem 4.4. The Brier score of the ensemble $\bar{f}$ can be estimated by  

$$
\begin{array}{r}{\mathbb{E}_{X\sim p_{X}}[\phi_{B}(\bar{f}(X))]=\mathbb{E}_{F_{1},F_{2}\sim p_{F}}\left[\mathbb{E}_{X\sim p_{X}}[F_{1}(X)F_{2}(X)]\right]-2\mathbb{E}_{F\sim p_{F}}[\mathbb{E}_{X\sim p_{X}}[F(X)]]+1}\end{array}
$$  

Due to the distributional equivalence across all models, we can estimate this by $f^{(1)},f^{(2)}\in\mathcal{F}$ :  

$$
\mathbb{E}_{X\sim p_{X}}[\phi_{B}(\bar{f}(X))]\approx\mathbb{E}_{X\sim p_{X}}[f^{(1)}({\pmb x})f^{(2)}({\pmb x})]-2\mathbb{E}_{X\sim p_{X}}[f^{(1)}({\pmb x})]+1
$$  

This is verified empirically in fig. 4 by using all 9900 possible pairs to estimate the ensemble performance. The mean and standard deviation of the estimation are visualized as the regions, while the solid curves represent the loss of the ensembles. Therefore, we can use only two models to accurately estimate the asymptotic loss of deep ensembles. Using this scheme, practitioners can make judicious and efficient choices in the application of deep ensembles and balance the trade-off between training budget and performance requirements.  

Bias-Variance Trade-Off of Ensembles. Another intriguing yet unsolved phenomenon of deep ensembles is its bias-variance trade-off as the single-model capacity of each ensemble member increases (Geiger et al., 2020; Kobayashi et al., 2021). Note that the ensemble performance under the Brier score can be written as a function of $\rho\,=\,\mathbb{E}_{X}[f(X)]$ for any $f\ \in\mathcal{F}$ as $S(\rho)\,=$ $\mathbb{E}_{X}[\bar{f}(X)^{2}]-2\underline{{\backslash}}_{X}[\bar{f}(X)]\overset{*}{+}1\overset{\dotsc}{=}\rho^{2}-2\overset{*}{\rho}\overset{*}{+}1+\operatorname{Var}_{X}[\breve{f}(X)]$ . From fig. 5, we know that $\operatorname{Var}_{X}[{\bar{f}}(X)]$ tend to increase with $\rho$ . Thus the Brier score $S(\boldsymbol{\rho})$ starts increasing before $\rho$ reaches 1 (as the single-model capacity increases). This leads to the bias-variance trade-off observed for deep ensembles.  



\section{5 ENSEMBLES OF LIMITED BUDGETS.  }


![](images/a66f60309d7f3dd68037458109b64462161ae21278b168999855ec70c455bee2.jpg)  

In practice, an approximation with $M$ models is utilized. It has been observed in many existing works that the loss decreases as $M$ increases. This phenomenon has not been understood either. Previous work simply terms $M$ as the “ensemble capacity”, which does not contribute to the understanding of the internal mechanism given the $M$ models are trained completely independently. It is also observed that increasing the ensemble capacity yields similar performance improvement compared with increasing the capacity of a single model, as shown in fig.  

Figure 5: An illustration of the relation between $\mathbb{E}_{X}[{\bar{f}}(X)]$ and $\operatorname{Var}_{X}[{\bar{f}}(X)]$ . All six structuredataset combinations are tested here. $\rho$ is controlled by the singlemodel capacity $k$ of ensemble members. For each curve, $\rho$ increases as the width parameter $k$ .  

8:  

Theorem 5.1. The expected Brier score loss of $M$ ensemble members is determined by  

$$
\begin{array}{r l}{\mathbb{E}_{F_{1},\cdots,F_{M}\sim\hat{p}_{F}}\Big[\phi_{B}\big(\frac{1}{M}\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{M}F_{i}(X)\big)\Big]=\mathbb{E}_{X\sim p_{X}}\big[\phi_{B}(\bar{\hat{f}}(X))\big]+\frac{\mathbb{E}_{X\sim p_{X}}\big[\operatorname{Var}_{F\sim\hat{p}_{F}}(F(x))\big]}{M}}&{}\\ {=\displaystyle\frac{M+1}{M}\mathbb{E}_{X\sim p_{X}}[\bar{\hat{f}}(X)^{2}]-\frac{\rho^{2}}{M}-2\rho+1}&{}\end{array}
$$  

The expected NLL of $M$ ensemble members is estimated by  

$$
\mathbb{E}_{F_{1},\cdots,F_{M}\sim\hat{p}_{F}}\Big[\phi_{n l l}\big(\frac{1}{M}\sum_{i=1}^{M}F_{i}(X)\big)\Big]\approx\mathbb{E}_{X\sim p_{X}}\big[\phi_{n l l}\big(\bar{\hat{f}}(X)\big)\big]+\mathbb{E}_{X\sim p_{X}}\Big[\frac{1-\bar{\hat{f}}(X)}{2\bar{\hat{f}}(X)}\Big]\frac{1}{M}
$$  

This theorem explains the constantly observed phenomenon where increasing the number of ensembles results in improved performance. Demonstrated by eqs. (12) and (14), the loss can be decomposed to the loss of the convergence result of the population means and another positive term controlled by $M$ . Thus the loss decreases in an inverse-proportional manner as we increase the number $M$ of ensemble members, i.e. the ensemble capacity. As $M\to\infty$ , the loss converges to the global loss of the population ensemble. Note that all values are determined by $p_{F}$ except for the ensemble budget $M$ . As a consequence, this theorem presents a closed-form expression for the performance of deep ensembles.e., the prediction of a single model. This theoretically verifies that both single-model capacity and ensemble capacity contribute to performance improvement but in  

![](images/07050d69164dcb1bd72354372a44ac86018a46d3ca343e056d21233f5d3761eb.jpg)  

Figure 6: The performance comparison between the scaling of a single model (black dashed curves) and increasing the number of ensemble members (colorful solid curves). In each ensemble (i.e. each colorful curve), $M$ varies from 1 to 100. The results are generated using CNN models.  

![](images/2fc41f86a8896aa1bcfeab477b0f661f0d5c60eefc1cea59238b1e8578431b48.jpg)  
Figure 7: The verification of theorem 5.1. The theoretical results (dashed curves) are compared with the empirical results of ensembles using $M$ models for (a)(b) the Brier score and (c)(d) the negative log-likelihood. The Brier score uses $\hat{p}_{F}$ while NLL uses $p_{F}$ . Results are generated using CNNs.  

completely distinct ways. We validate these theoretical results in fig. 7, where the empirical loss and the theoretical decomposition are compared. Note that although the theorems are derived for $\hat{\mathcal F}$ with complete neural collapse, it generalizes to $\mathcal{F}$ due to the already existing neural collapse nature in trained models. Therefore the experiments are also carried out for $\mathcal{F}$ to demonstrate the practical significance. It is observed that the curves almost completely overlap. The estimation of NLL deviates a little at the beginning due to Taylor’s expansion approximation. Here we clip $(1-{\bar{\hat{f}}}(X))/(2{\bar{\hat{f}}}(X))$ by an upper bound of 5 to avoid explosion caused by difficult samples to avoid overflow issues.  

Besides, inspired by theorem 4.4, the Brier score can be rewritten as eq. (13), where the first term can be estimated by the agreement between two single models. We thus estimate the entire curve using only two models, and plot the standard deviation as the colorful regions in fig. 7(a)(b). This provides even further and deeper understanding compared with fig. 4.  



\section{6 CONCLUSIONS  }


In this work, we focus on the important problem of demystifying the “mysterious effectiveness” of deep ensembles. We diagnose and demonstrate the problem of existing ways of understanding deep ensembles and the inconsistency in practice. Then we reveal the distributional equivalence property of model distributions through comprehensive experimental results. Based on such a property, we provide theoretical analysis with rigorously proved theorems to (1) demonstrate the guarantee of deep ensembles’ effectiveness; (2) propose a scheme to accurately estimate the asymptotic performance of infinitely many models using only two models; and (3) lucidly uncover the essence of increasing the ensemble capacity and scaling up a single model. In conclusion, our work discloses the true mechanism of mysterious deep ensembles for the first time. The derivations also provide valuable insights into the understanding of the behaviors of models through the perspective of the joint distribution between data and models. We admit that the distributional equivalence property is an observational conclusion. It remains an intriguing question why the probability measure of the testing data mastered by any single model retains an identical level across different models. We deduce it’s due to certain properties of distribution shift and leave that for future explorations.

content.summary: This paper develops a theory to explain why homogeneous deep ensemble can improve the performance.
content.strengths: - The experiments of distributional equivalence property in Sections 4.1 and 4.2 are interesting.
content.weaknesses:
- The setting and assumptions of the distribution $p_F$ which is crucial are not clear. All mentioned is $F \sim p_F$ is a trained model. I believe that this is important for the solidness of the developed theory. At least, we should have something to connect the min over $\mathcal{F}$ and the member function $F \sim p_F$. Otherwise, the theory is loosing. 
- The proof of Proposition 3.1 can not be found.
-  It seems to me that the definition of $\hat{f}(x)$ is not meaningful because it is associated with a function $f \in \mathcal{F}$ and it returns 1 if $f(x)$ is a correct prediction and $0$ otherwise. Therefore, $E_{F\sim \hat{p}_F}[F(X)]$ seems to be the percentage of the number of correct predictions when $F \sim p_F$. What is the meaning of applying $\phi$ to this average. Similar question to $\phi(\hat{f}(x))$ in Eq. (8).
-  I have checked the proof of Lemma B1 and Theorem 4.1. They require $E_{x \sim p_x}[\hat{f}(x)]$ = $E_{x \sim p_x}[\hat{g}(x)]$ for every $\hat{f}, \hat{g} \in \hat{\mathcal{F}}$ or the accuracies are equal for every $f,g \in \mathcal{F}$ which is weird for a general function space $\mathcal{F}$. Certainly, we can partly assume this with $f,g \sim p_F$ which is the distribution of well-trained models. However, the support of $p_F$ is only a subset of $\mathcal{F}$. Therefore, I reckon that the minimization in Theorem 4.1 should be over the support of $p_F$.
content.questions: Please address my questions in Weaknesses.
content.weakness:
### weaknesses:
 - The setting and assumptions of the distribution $p_F$ are not rigorously defined. The paper states that $F \sim p_F$ is a trained model, but it lacks a precise definition of what constitutes a "trained model" in this context. For example, is there a specific loss threshold or accuracy level that a model must achieve to be considered part of the set of trained models? Without a clear definition of the set of models $F$ and the distribution $p_F$, it is difficult to assess the validity of the theoretical framework. Specifically, the connection between the minimization over $\mathcal{F}$ and the member function $F \sim p_F$ remains unclear, potentially weakening the theory.
- The proof of Proposition 3.1 is missing. Additionally, the derivation $\min_{f \in F} \phi(f(x)) = \phi(\min_{f \in F} f(x))$ in the proposition's statement is incorrect for monotonically decreasing functions, meaning the proposition in its current form only applies to monotonically increasing functions. This limitation should be addressed.
-  The definition of $\hat{f}(x)$ appears to be oversimplified. It is defined as returning 1 if $f(x)$ is a correct prediction and 0 otherwise. However, the subsequent use of $\phi(\hat{f}(x))$ and $E_{F\sim \hat{p}_F}[F(X)]$ in equations like Eq. (8) is not well-justified. The paper needs to clarify the meaning of applying the function $\phi$ to the average of these binary values, which essentially represents the accuracy of a model. Furthermore, the assumption that the predicted probability collapses to a one-hot vector is quite strong and requires more empirical evidence. While the authors mention the neural collapse phenomenon, conducting experiments on their trained models to verify this assumption would strengthen the paper.
- The proof of Lemma B1 and Theorem 4.1 relies on the assumption that $E_{x \sim p_x}[\hat{f}(x)] = E_{x \sim p_x}[\hat{g}(x)]$ for every $\hat{f}, \hat{g} \in \hat{\mathcal{F}}$. This implies that the accuracies are equal for every $f, g \in \mathcal{F}$, which is a strong and potentially unrealistic assumption for a general function space $\mathcal{F}$. While this might be partially justified by considering $f, g \sim p_F$ as well-trained models, the support of $p_F$ is only a subset of $\mathcal{F}$. Consequently, the minimization in Theorem 4.1 should arguably be restricted to the support of $p_F$ to ensure its validity. Moreover, the distributional equivalence of $\hat{p}(F)$ cannot be directly inherited from $p_F$ without a rigorous proof, and its implication that models sampled from $p_F$ have the same accuracy over the general data/label distribution raises questions about the relationship between well-trained models and their accuracies. The paper should clarify whether Figures 2, 8, and 9 pertain to $f \sim p_F$ or $\hat{f} \sim p_{\hat{F}}$.
- In Line 152, the objective function $min_f E_{(x,y) \in {X_{train} \times Y_{train}}}[...]$ is not standard notation and could be confusing. It should be clarified.
content.suggestions:
To improve the paper's theoretical grounding, I suggest a more rigorous definition of the set of trained models $\mathcal{F}$ and the distribution $p_F$. The authors could define a trained model as one that minimizes the empirical loss below a certain threshold or achieves a specific accuracy level on the training set. This would provide a concrete criterion for determining whether a model belongs to $\mathcal{F}$ or can be considered as sampled from $p_F$. Furthermore, the paper should elaborate on the relationship between $\mathcal{F}$ and $p_F$, clarifying how the distribution $p_F$ is derived from the set of trained models. This could involve discussing the optimization algorithm used for training and how its stochasticity leads to a distribution over the space of trained models. Providing such details would enhance the clarity and rigor of the theoretical framework, making it easier for readers to understand and evaluate the paper's claims.

In addition to the above, the paper would benefit from a more thorough discussion of the distributional equivalence property and its implications. While the experiments in Sections 4.1 and 4.2 are interesting, the paper should provide a more rigorous proof of how this property is inherited by the surrogate models $\hat{f}$ from the original models $f$. This could involve demonstrating that the transformation from $f$ to $\hat{f}$ preserves the distributional equivalence under certain conditions. Moreover, the paper should address the implications of this property for the accuracy of the models. If the distributional equivalence of $\hat{p}_F$ implies that models sampled from $p_F$ have the same accuracy, the paper should discuss how this relates to the concept of well-trained models and their performance on unseen data. It would be helpful to clarify whether the observed distributional equivalence is a consequence of the specific training procedure or a more general property of deep neural networks. Providing a more in-depth analysis of these aspects would strengthen the paper's theoretical contributions and enhance its overall impact.

Finally, the authors should revisit the definition and usage of $\hat{f}(x)$. While the idea of simplifying the model's output to a binary value is understandable, the paper needs to justify the application of the function $\phi$ to the average of these binary values. One possible approach could be to interpret $\phi$ as a measure of the model's confidence or uncertainty in its prediction. In this case, applying $\phi$ to the average accuracy could be seen as quantifying the overall confidence of the ensemble. Alternatively, the authors could consider using a different function that operates directly on the model's output probabilities, rather than the simplified binary values. This would avoid the need for the strong assumption that the predicted probability collapses to a one-hot vector. Additionally, the paper should provide more empirical evidence to support this assumption, such as conducting experiments on the trained models to measure the distribution of their output probabilities. Addressing these issues would improve the clarity and rigor of the paper's methodology and make it more convincing to the readers.

content.summary:
This paper challenges the common explanation for deep ensemble success, which attributes their effectiveness to Jensen’s inequality. The authors argue that this explanation is insufficient and propose a new perspective based on the **distributional equivalence property** of trained models. According to this view, although ensemble members may differ in their individual predictions, their output distributions (based on the predicted probability for the target class) are statistically identical across the dataset.

Through both theoretical analysis and empirical evidence, the paper demonstrates how this distributional equivalence property helps explain why deep ensembles consistently outperform single models. Additionally, the authors introduce estimation schemes to predict ensemble performance based on the number of members and model capacity, offering insights into the relationship between ensemble capacity and single-model scaling.
content.strengths:
- The paper offers practical insights into the relationship between global diversity and ensemble performance improvement, as well as the distinct contributions of ensemble capacity versus single-model scaling. The estimation schemes, which allow practitioners to approximate ensemble performance using just two models, provide a computationally efficient approach that could inform more cost-effective system design.
- Beyond merely acknowledging the diversity of ensemble members, the paper provides an in-depth analysis of both **point-wise** and **global diversity**, emphasizing the latter as a key factor often overlooked in prior research.
content.weaknesses:
- The main contribution, introducing distributional equivalence, may lack generalizability to other loss functions. Please refer to the question section for more details.
- The distributional equivalence condition, a foundational assumption underlying all theorems, seems overly restrictive. Specifically, in the proof of Theorem B.1, lines 692–695, the authors assert that “for arbitrary $\(\hat{f}, \hat{g} \in \hat{F}, P(\hat{f}(x)) = P(\hat{g}(x))\)$” holds  $\(\forall x \in X\)$, which is a strong assumption that I am still not convinced.
content.questions:
1. Section 4.1’s findings on distributional equivalence focus on the predicted probability for the target class, without considering the full class probability distribution. Could the authors provide an additional analysis that incorporates more information, such as the difference $\(l = p(y) - p(\hat{y})\)$, where $\(\hat{y}\)$ is the predicted class and $\(p(\hat{y})\)$ is the highest probability across all classes? This could give a broader perspective on the consistency of distributional equivalence.

2. Which loss function was used to train the models in Section 4.1? My hypothesis is that the loss function has a significant influence on these findings. For instance, cross-entropy loss focuses on the probability of the target class, so models trained with this objective might naturally exhibit distributional equivalence on this specific metric. In contrast, a loss function such as label smoothing, which considers probabilities across all classes, might lead to different observations, potentially limiting the generalizability of the results in Section 4.1. Could the authors discuss or provide empirical insights on how these findings might vary with alternative loss functions?
content.weakness:
- The main contribution, introducing distributional equivalence, may lack generalizability to other loss functions. Please refer to the question section for more details.
- The distributional equivalence condition, a foundational assumption underlying all theorems, seems overly restrictive. Specifically, in the proof of Theorem B.1, lines 692–695, the authors assert that “for arbitrary $\(\hat{f}, \hat{g} \in \hat{F}, P(\hat{f}(x)) = P(\hat{g}(x))\)$” holds  $\(\forall x \in X\)$, which is a strong assumption that I am still not convinced. This assumption, while perhaps empirically observed under specific conditions, needs further justification regarding its applicability across diverse training scenarios and model architectures. The core issue is that the assumption of identical output distributions for all models in the ensemble, even when trained independently, is not inherently guaranteed by the training process itself. It is possible that models converge to different local minima, resulting in distinct output distributions, especially when using different random initializations or training data orderings. The paper does not adequately address this potential variability, which could significantly impact the validity of the theoretical results.
content.suggestions:
The paper's analysis of distributional equivalence, while insightful, would benefit from a more thorough exploration of its limitations. Specifically, the assumption that $P(\hat{f}(x)) = P(\hat{g}(x))$ for all models $\hat{f}$ and $\hat{g}$ and all inputs $x$ is a strong one that requires more rigorous justification. While the authors present empirical evidence supporting this claim, they should also investigate scenarios where this assumption might break down. For example, they could explore the impact of different training regimes, such as varying learning rates or batch sizes, on the degree of distributional equivalence. Additionally, it would be valuable to examine the behavior of this property when using different model architectures or datasets with varying levels of complexity. A more nuanced analysis of the conditions under which distributional equivalence holds, and the extent to which it can be relied upon, would significantly strengthen the paper's conclusions. Furthermore, the authors should consider providing a theoretical analysis of the sensitivity of their results to deviations from this assumption. This could involve deriving bounds on the performance of ensembles when the distributional equivalence property is only approximately satisfied, which would provide a more realistic and robust understanding of their findings.

To address the concern about the loss function's influence, the authors should conduct experiments using a broader range of loss functions beyond cross-entropy. For instance, they could explore the behavior of distributional equivalence when using loss functions like mean squared error (MSE) or label smoothing. These alternative loss functions might lead to different convergence behaviors and could potentially challenge the observed distributional equivalence. It would be particularly interesting to see if the distributional equivalence property still holds when the loss function does not directly focus on the probability of the target class. If the property does not generalize to other loss functions, the authors should clearly state this limitation and discuss its implications for the broader applicability of their results. This would provide a more complete picture of the conditions under which the proposed theory is valid and help to identify potential areas for future research. The authors could also consider analyzing the gradients of different loss functions with respect to the model parameters to understand how they might influence the convergence behavior and the resulting output distributions.

Finally, the paper should provide more practical guidance on how to assess the degree of distributional equivalence in practice. While the authors present some empirical results, they do not offer a clear methodology for practitioners to determine whether this property holds for their specific models and datasets. It would be valuable to introduce a metric or a set of diagnostics that can be used to quantify the extent to which the output distributions of different models in an ensemble are equivalent. This would allow practitioners to make informed decisions about whether to rely on the theoretical results presented in the paper. Furthermore, the authors could explore the potential for using techniques like adversarial training or data augmentation to promote distributional equivalence, which could lead to improved ensemble performance. This would provide a more practical and actionable contribution to the field of deep learning.

content.summary: The paper discovers that members of homogeneous ensembles have the distributional equivalence property. Based on this property, the paper answers the fundamental question that why deep ensembles work. Additionally, a way to estimate the performance of deep ensembles is proposed. The paper further discusses the bias-variance tradeoff in deep ensembles and compares the effectiveness of scaling a single model versus scaling deep ensembles. Comprehensive theoretical and empirical analyses are provided to support the claims in the paper.
content.strengths:
1. Theoretical findings are fruitful and solid.
2. Insightful discussions about related works can effectively engage readers.
content.weaknesses:
1. In Line 135 of the paper, aurthors mention that the unsatisfactory results of the majority vote classifier inspire them to explore overlooked conditions, which turns out to be the distributional equivalence property. Then, why is there no further effort to explain the majority vote classifier after finding the overlooked condition?
2. The distributional equivalence property is observed on CIFAR-10 dataset. However, on such small datasets, it's known that the trained DNNs tend to be quite overconfident, especially in the case where weak or even no regularization is adopted. Combined with the fact that DNNs are highly accurate on small datasets, the observed distributional equivalence is not surprising at all. To justify the distributional 
equivalence property in general cases, larger datasets and/or stronger regularization settings need to be considered. 
3. In Section 4.4, results are mainly derived for the Brier score, while those for NLL are missing.
4. In Fig. 6, it's better to include plots for error rate in additon to losses.
5. In Line 676, authors note that SGD is used without momentum or data augmentation. However, these techiques are crucial for DNNs to reach high performance. If they are not considered, the results of the paper could have limited practicality.
content.questions: Please see the weakness part.
content.weakness:
1. In Line 135 of the paper, aurthors mention that the unsatisfactory results of the majority vote classifier inspire them to explore overlooked conditions, which turns out to be the distributional equivalence property. Then, why is there no further effort to explain the majority vote classifier after finding the overlooked condition?
2. The distributional equivalence property is observed on CIFAR-10 dataset. However, on such small datasets, it's known that the trained DNNs tend to be quite overconfident, especially in the case where weak or even no regularization is adopted. Combined with the fact that DNNs are highly accurate on small datasets, the observed distributional equivalence is not surprising at all. To justify the distributional 
equivalence property in general cases, larger datasets and/or stronger regularization settings need to be considered. 
3. In Section 4.4, results are mainly derived for the Brier score, while those for NLL are missing.
4. In Fig. 6, it's better to include plots for error rate in additon to losses.
5. In Line 676, authors note that SGD is used without momentum or data augmentation. However, these techiques are crucial for DNNs to reach high performance. If they are not considered, the results of the paper could have limited practicality.
content.suggestions:
The paper's focus on the distributional equivalence property is interesting, but its reliance on overconfident models trained on small datasets raises concerns about the generalizability of the findings. The authors should investigate the behavior of this property under more realistic conditions, such as with larger datasets and stronger regularization techniques. Specifically, experiments should be conducted on datasets like ImageNet or similar, where models are less prone to overfitting and overconfidence. Furthermore, it would be beneficial to explore the impact of various regularization methods, including dropout, weight decay, and batch normalization, on the observed distributional equivalence. This would help to determine whether the property is a general phenomenon or an artifact of the specific training setup used in the paper. The current analysis, while mathematically sound, lacks the empirical breadth to support its claims in more practical scenarios. 

Furthermore, the paper's analysis is heavily focused on the Brier score, while neglecting the Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL), which is a more commonly used metric for evaluating probabilistic predictions. While the Brier score is useful for analyzing variance, the NLL provides a more direct measure of the quality of the predicted probabilities. The authors should include a thorough analysis of the NLL, both theoretically and empirically, to provide a more complete picture of the ensemble performance. This should include derivations similar to those presented for the Brier score, as well as experimental results on the same datasets. Additionally, the paper should include error rate plots in Fig. 6, as suggested, to provide a more comprehensive view of the model's performance. The error rate is a more intuitive measure of performance, and it would be helpful to see how it correlates with the observed loss trends.

Finally, the training strategy used in the paper, which excludes momentum and data augmentation, is a significant limitation. These techniques are standard practice in deep learning and are crucial for achieving high performance on many tasks. The authors should conduct experiments using these techniques to ensure that the observed distributional equivalence property is not specific to the simplified training setup. This would involve training models with different optimizers, such as Adam or SGD with momentum, and with various data augmentation strategies. The paper should also investigate the impact of these techniques on the ensemble performance and the distributional equivalence property. By addressing these issues, the paper could significantly enhance the practical relevance and generalizability of its findings.

content.summary: Previous theory of Deep Ensemble owing the benefit of ensembling to the diversity of ensemble members with "trivial" Jasen Gap of the losses without considering the data distribution. Instead, this paper introduces the distributional equivalence of the trained members from global data distribution, which further explains Deep Ensemble works with the equivalent output distribution globally but with distinct sample-wise predictions for a group of samples. Under this perspective, the paper further provides the theory of how sample-wise diversity and the number of members contribute to the performance. Besides, it provides a method to predict the ensemble performance with only two models.
content.strengths:
1. The paper is easy to follow and well-organized. 
2. The introduced "distributional equivalence" that all members produce similar prediction distribution across a group of samples is novel, and has been neglected by previous works.
3. The theory is technically sound and the prediction of ensemble performance with only two models is also interesting.
content.weaknesses:
1. What does the "only differ in the training stochasticity" actually mean? i.e., where does stochasticity come from? from the shuffled dataset? or random initialization?
2. An interesting question is why the homogeneous members demonstrate the "distributional equivalence" property. Does it come from the fixed dataset bias, fixed architecture, etc.?
content.questions: See Weaknesses.
content.weakness:
1. What does the "only differ in the training stochasticity" actually mean? i.e., where does stochasticity come from? from the shuffled dataset? or random initialization?
2. An interesting question is why the homogeneous members demonstrate the "distributional equivalence" property. Does it come from the fixed dataset bias, fixed architecture, etc.?
content.suggestions:
The paper introduces the concept of 'distributional equivalence' in deep ensembles, which is a valuable perspective. However, the paper could benefit from a more rigorous exploration of the factors contributing to this phenomenon. Specifically, the authors should delve deeper into the mechanisms that lead to the observed output distribution similarity across ensemble members. For instance, a more detailed analysis of how different random seeds for initialization and shuffling of the training data affect the final output distributions would be beneficial. It would be interesting to see if specific architectural choices or optimization algorithms amplify or diminish this effect. Furthermore, the paper could explore whether the distributional equivalence is a property that holds across different types of datasets, or if it is more pronounced in certain data distributions. This would provide a more complete understanding of the scope and limitations of the proposed theory.

To strengthen the theoretical foundation, the authors could consider incorporating concepts from the field of generalization theory. For example, the notion of 'flat minima' in the loss landscape, which is often associated with better generalization, could be related to the observed distributional equivalence. It would be interesting to investigate whether models that converge to flatter minima exhibit stronger distributional equivalence. Furthermore, the paper could explore the relationship between the sharpness of the loss landscape and the diversity of the ensemble members. This could provide insights into how the optimization process shapes the output distributions and how it contributes to the overall performance of the ensemble. A more thorough analysis of these aspects would significantly enhance the theoretical contribution of the paper.

Finally, the paper could benefit from a more detailed discussion of the practical implications of the proposed theory. While the method to predict ensemble performance with only two models is interesting, the paper should provide more guidance on how to apply this method in practice. For example, it would be helpful to discuss the computational cost of this method and how it scales with the size of the ensemble. Furthermore, the paper could explore the limitations of this method and under which conditions it is most effective. A more thorough discussion of these practical aspects would make the paper more accessible and useful to the broader research community.

content.summary: This paper examines why deep ensembles are effective by proposing distributional equivalence, the idea that ensemble members produce predictions with identical distributions. The authors argue this property is key to the performance gains of deep ensembles, offering a new explanation beyond the Jensen gap. They validate this theory through experiments and introduce an estimation scheme that allows ensemble performance to be predicted using just two models, reducing evaluation costs and highlighting the unique benefits of deep ensembles over scaling single models.
content.strengths:
1. This paper introduces a fresh theoretical perspective on deep ensembles by proposing the concept of distributional equivalence among ensemble members. This approach offers an alternative to the usual Jensen gap explanation, providing insight into why deep ensembles work by analyzing homogeneous model distributions.
2. The proposed method of estimating ensemble performance using only two models is a practical contribution that could streamline the application of ensembles in resource-constrained settings.
3. The authors have conducted comprehensive experiments across multiple datasets and architectures, providing strong empirical support for their theoretical claims.
content.weaknesses:
1. The theoretical framework relies on assumptions about neural collapse, which may not apply universally across various datasets and model configurations. This reliance could limit the generalizability of the findings, especially in cases with limited data or high variability.
2.Deep ensembles are frequently applied to enhance uncertainty estimation, the paper mainly emphasizes performance improvements, leaving the effect of distributional equivalence on uncertainty quantification undiscussed.
content.questions:
1. The experiments have included the CIFAR-10 and TinyImageNet, which may not represent the complexity of real-world data, such as text data or multi-modal data. Could the authors discuss how dataset complexity might affect the distributional equivalence property, especially for datasets with higher-dimensional or more variable features?
2. How sensitive is the distributional equivalence property to various training hyperparameters, such as learning rate or initialization seed? An analysis of this could help in understanding the robustness of it across different training configurations.
3. The paper introduces a method for performance estimation using two models. Could the authors comment on the potential for scaling this approach for ensembles of significantly larger capacity.
content.weakness:
1. The theoretical framework relies on assumptions about neural collapse, which may not apply universally across various datasets and model configurations. This reliance could limit the generalizability of the findings, especially in cases with limited data or high variability. Specifically, the assumption that models fully interpolate the training data and exhibit confident predictions might not hold for datasets where the signal-to-noise ratio is low or the data distribution is highly complex. In such scenarios, models might not converge to a state of neural collapse, potentially invalidating the distributional equivalence property.
2.Deep ensembles are frequently applied to enhance uncertainty estimation, the paper mainly emphasizes performance improvements, leaving the effect of distributional equivalence on uncertainty quantification undiscussed. This is a significant oversight, as the quality of uncertainty estimates is crucial in many real-world applications, such as medical diagnosis or autonomous driving. The paper should investigate how the proposed distributional equivalence impacts the diversity of predictions, which is a key factor in reliable uncertainty quantification.
content.suggestions:
To strengthen the paper, the authors should investigate the sensitivity of their distributional equivalence property to various dataset characteristics. Specifically, they should explore how the property behaves when applied to datasets with varying levels of noise, different dimensionalities, and varying degrees of class imbalance. For instance, analyzing the behavior on datasets with high levels of label noise would be valuable, as neural collapse might not occur in such cases, leading to a breakdown of the distributional equivalence. Furthermore, the authors should consider datasets with significantly higher dimensionality than CIFAR-10 or TinyImageNet, such as those found in natural language processing or multi-modal learning, to assess the generalizability of their findings. This analysis should include a quantitative evaluation of the distributional equivalence property under these different conditions, potentially using metrics like the Wasserstein distance or other appropriate measures to quantify the similarity of the prediction distributions.

In addition, the paper should address the implications of distributional equivalence for uncertainty quantification. The authors should investigate how the proposed property affects the diversity of predictions across ensemble members, which is crucial for reliable uncertainty estimation. This could be done by analyzing the variance of predictions for individual data points and correlating this variance with the confidence of the ensemble. A detailed analysis of how the distributional equivalence impacts the ensemble's ability to capture aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty would be essential. Furthermore, the authors should explore whether the distributional equivalence property leads to overconfident predictions in certain scenarios, and if so, how this can be mitigated. This analysis would significantly enhance the practical relevance of the paper, as uncertainty estimation is a key application of deep ensembles.

Finally, the authors should provide a more detailed discussion of the limitations of their proposed method for estimating ensemble performance using only two models. While this approach is computationally efficient, it might not accurately capture the behavior of larger ensembles, especially when the individual models are not perfectly distributionally equivalent. The authors should explore the conditions under which this two-model estimation is reliable and provide guidelines for its application. They could also investigate whether the estimation accuracy improves with the use of more than two models, and if so, what the trade-offs are between computational cost and estimation accuracy. A more thorough analysis of the limitations and potential biases of the proposed estimation method would be crucial for its practical adoption.

papers.paper_text:
\title{Gradient-Free Analytical Fisher Information of Diffused Distributions}

\begin{abstract}
Diffusion models (DMs) have demonstrated powerful distributional modeling capabilities by matching the first-order score of diffused distributions.
Recent advancements have explored incorporating the second-order Fisher information, defined as the negative Hessian of log-density, into various downstream tasks and theoretical analysis of DMs.
However, current practices often overlook the inherent structure of diffused distributions, accessing Fisher information via applying auto-differentiation to the learned score network. 
This approach, while straightforward, leaves theoretical properties unexplored and is time-consuming. 
In this paper, we derive the analytical formulation of Fisher information (AFI) by applying consecutive differentials to the diffused distributions.
As a result, AFI takes a gradient-free form of a weighted sum (or integral) of outer-products of the score and initial data.
Based on this formulation, we propose two algorithmic variants of AFI for distinct scenarios.
When evaluating the AFI’s trace, we introduce a parameterized network to learn the trace.
When AFI is applied as a linear operator, we present a training-free method that simplifies it into several inner-product calculations.
Furthermore, we provide theoretical guarantees for both algorithms regarding convergence analysis and approximation error bounds.
Additionally, we leverage AFI to establish the first general theorem for the optimal transport property of the diffusion ODE deduced map.
Experiments in likelihood evaluation and adjoint optimization demonstrate the superior accuracy and reduced time-cost of the proposed algorithms.
\end{abstract}

\section{1 INTRODUCTION  }


The emerging diffusion models (DMs) Sohl-Dickstein et al. (2015); Ho et al. (2020); Song & Ermon (2019); Song et al. (2020), generating samples of data distribution from initial noise by learning a reverse diffusion process, have been proven to be an effective technique for modeling data distribution, especially in generating high-quality images Nichol et al. (2022); Dhariwal & Nichol (2021a); Saharia et al. (2022); Ramesh et al. (2022); Rombach et al. (2022); Ho et al. (2022). The training process of DMs can be seen as employing a neural network to match the first-order score $\nabla_{\pmb{x}}\log q_{t}(\pmb{x})$ of the diffused distributions at varying noise levels.  

Recently, there has been a growing trend to recognize the importance of the Fisher information in DMs, defined as the negative Hessian of the diffused distributions’ log-density, $-\nabla_{x}^{2}\log q_{t}(x)$ . The Fisher information provides valuable second-order information of DMs and plays a crucial role in likelihood evaluation (Lu et al., 2022a; Zheng et al., 2023), adjoint optimization (Pan et al., 2023a;b; Blasingame & Liu, 2024), and optimal transport analysis Zhang et al. (2024a).  

However, current practices (Sanchez et al., 2022; Song & Lai, 2024) typically overlook the inherent structure of diffused distributions, and access the Fisher information by applying auto-differentiation to the score network. While this is a straightforward approach, it leads to time-consuming gradient operations, even with the help of the Jacobian-vector-product (JVP) technique. Moreover, in the likelihood evaluation task, the current JVP method still needs a quadratic order time complexity concerning the dimension to calculate the trace of the Fisher information, rendering likelihood evaluation intractable for SD-level models. Additionally, due to a lack of comprehensive understanding of Fisher Information, Zhang et al. (2024a) have to impose stringent assumptions to characterize the optimal transport property of the diffusion ODE deduced map.  

In this paper, we delve deeper into the inherent quadratic structure of diffused distributions and derive the analytical form of Fisher information (AFI) by applying the consecutive partial differential chain rule to the marginal distributions. Notice that, while this inherent structure has been consistently utilized in the learning process of the score network, it has often been overlooked when accessing Fisher Information up until now. The main contributions of our paper are listed as follows:  

• We develop the first analytical formulation of the Fisher Information (AFI) of diffused distributions, which is gradient-free. Initially, we show that the AFI manifests as a weighted summation of outer-products of the score and initial data when the initial distribution is a sum of Dirac. We then extend this result to an integral form in a more general setting. The AFI suggests a theoretical possibility of accessing Fisher information without needing costly gradient calculations in practice.   
• Based on the AFI, we propose two algorithmic alternatives to the JVP, each tailored to different types of Fisher Information access. For the evaluation of Fisher information’s trace, we introduce a parameterized network to learn the trace, significantly reducing the time complexity of trace evaluation from quadratic to linear w.r.t. the dimension. In scenarios where Fisher Information is applied as a linear operator, we present a training-free method that simplifies the complex linear transformation calculations into several simple innerproduct calculations. Furthermore, we provide theoretical guarantees for these algorithms, including convergence analysis and approximation error bounds.   
• Utilizing the analytical knowledge of the Fisher information, we establish the first theorem that allows the general diffusion ODE deduced mapping to possess the optimal transport property, eliminating the need for stringent assumptions.  

We evaluate our AFI algorithms on likelihood evaluation and adjoint optimization tasks. The empirical results demonstrate the enhanced accuracy and reduced time-cost of our AFI methods.  



\section{2 PRELIMINARIES  }


Notation. The Euclidean norm over $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ is denoted by $\left\Vert\cdot\right\Vert$ , and the Euclidean inner product is denoted by $\langle\cdot|\cdot\rangle$ . Throughout, we simply write $\emph{\textbf{\textit{\textbf{f}}}}\!\!\!\operatorname{\textbf{\textit{\textbf{\textmu}}}}$ to denote the integral with respect to the Lebesgue measure: $\textstyle\int g(x)\mathrm{d}x$ . When the integral is with respect to a different measure $\mu$ , we explicitly write $\int g\mathrm{d}\mu$ . When clear from context, we sometimes abuse notation by identifying a measure $\mu$ with its Lebesgue density. We also use $\delta(\cdot)$ to denote the Dirac Delta function.  

2.1 DIFFUSION MODELS AND DIFFUSION SDES  

Suppose that we have a ${\mathrm{d}}\cdot$ -dimensional random variable $\pmb{x}_{0}\ \in\ \mathbb{R}^{d}$ following an unknown target distribution $q_{0}(x_{0})$ . Diffusion Models (DMs) define a forward process $\{\boldsymbol{x}_{t}\}_{t\in[0,T]}$ with $T\ >\ 0$ starting with $\scriptstyle x_{0}$ , such that the distribution of $\pmb{x}_{t}$ conditioned on $\pmb{x}_{0}$ satisfies  

$$
\begin{array}{r l}{\mathrm{(Diffusion~Transition~Kernel)}\quad}&{{}q_{t|0}(x_{t}|x_{0})=\mathcal{N}(x_{t};\alpha(t)x_{0},\sigma^{2}(t)\mathbf{I}),}\end{array}
$$  

where $\alpha(\cdot),\sigma(\cdot)\in\mathcal{C}([0,T]\,,\mathbb{R}^{+})$ have bounded derivatives, and we denote them as $\alpha_{t}$ and $\sigma_{t}$ for simplicity. The choice for $\alpha_{t}$ and $\sigma_{t}$ is referred to as the noise schedule of a DM. According to Kingma et al. (2021); Karras et al. (2022), with some assumption on $\alpha(\cdot)$ and $\sigma(\cdot)$ , the forward process can be modeled as a linear SDE which is also called Ornstein–Uhlenbeck process:  

$$
\begin{array}{r}{\mathrm{d}\pmb{x}_{t}=f(t)\pmb{x}_{t}\mathrm{d}t+g(t)\mathrm{d}B_{t},}\end{array}
$$  

where $B_{t}$ is the standard d-dimensional Brownian Motion (BM), $\begin{array}{r}{f(t)=\frac{\mathrm{d}\log\alpha_{t}}{\mathrm{d}t}}\end{array}$ and $\begin{array}{r}{g^{2}(t)=\frac{\mathrm{d}\sigma_{t}^{2}}{\mathrm{d}t}\;-}\end{array}$ 2 d lodgt αtσt2 . Under some regularity conditions, the above forward SDE equation 2 have a reverse SDE from time $T$ to $0$ , which starts from $\pmb{x}_{t}$ Anderson (1982):  

$$
\begin{array}{r}{\mathrm{d}\pmb{x}_{t}=\left[f(t)\pmb{x}_{t}-g^{2}(t)\nabla_{\pmb{x}_{t}}\log q(\pmb{x}_{t},t)\right]\mathrm{d}t+g(t)\mathrm{d}\tilde{B}_{t},}\end{array}
$$  

where ${\tilde{B}}_{t}$ is the reverse-time Brownian motion and $\boldsymbol{q}(\boldsymbol{x}_{t},t)$ is the single-time marginal distribution of the forward process. In practice, DMs Ho et al. (2020); Song et al. (2020) use $\pmb{\varepsilon}_{\theta}(\pmb{x}_{t},t)$ to estimate $-\sigma(t)\nabla_{\pmb{x}_{t}}\log\overline{{q(\mathbf{x}_{t},t)}}$ and the parameter $\theta$ is optimized by the following objective:  

$$
\theta^{*}=\arg\operatorname*{min}_{\theta}\mathbb{E}_{t}\left\{\lambda_{t}\mathbb{E}_{x_{0},x_{t}}\left[\|s_{\theta}(x_{t},t)-\nabla_{x_{t}}\log p(x_{t},t|x_{0},0)\|^{2}\right]\right\},
$$  

where $s_{\theta}$ represents the parameterized score, i.e., $\begin{array}{r}{s_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_{t},t)=-\frac{\varepsilon_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_{t},t)}{\sigma_{t}}}\end{array}$ . This familiar parameterization is called $\epsilon$ -prediction. There are also $\textit{\textbf{y}}$ -prediction and $\pmb{v}$ -prediction Salimans & Ho (2022). The corresponding loss is equal to replace the term $\vert\epsilon\,-\,\pmb{\varepsilon}_{\theta}(\pmb{x}_{t},t)\vert$ with $\begin{array}{r}{\frac{\alpha_{t}}{\sigma_{t}}|\mathbf{x}_{0}\,-\,\bar{\pmb y}_{\theta}(\pmb x_{t},t)|}\end{array}$ and $\vert\alpha_{t}\epsilon-\sigma_{t}\mathbf{x}_{0}-v_{\theta}(x_{t},t)\vert$ . The learned $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{\theta}(\boldsymbol{x}_{t},t)$ can be also transformed to a $\textit{\textbf{y}}$ -prediction form by y¯θ(xt, t) = xt−σtεθ(xt,t).  

2.2 DIFFUSION MODELS INFERENCE AS NEURAL ODE  

It is noted that the reverse diffusion SDE in equation 3 has an associated probability flow ODE (also called diffusion ODE), which is a deterministic process that shares the same single-time marginal distribution Song et al. (2020):  

$$
\left(\mathrm{PF-ODE}\right)\;\;\;\;\;\;\mathrm{d}x_{t}=\left[f(t)x_{t}-\frac{1}{2}g^{2}(t)\nabla_{x_{t}}\log q_{t}({\boldsymbol{x}}_{t},t)\right]\mathrm{d}t.
$$  

By replacing the score function in equation 5 with the noise predictor $\varepsilon_{\theta}$ , the inference process of DMs can be constructed by the following neural ODE:  

$$
{\frac{\mathrm{d}\mathbf{}x_{t}}{\mathrm{d}t}}=h_{\theta}\left(x_{t},t\right):=f(t)\mathbf{x}_{t}+{\frac{g^{2}(t)}{2\sigma_{t}}}\mathbf{\epsilon}_{\theta}\left(x_{t},t\right),\quad\mathbf{x}_{T}\sim{\mathcal{N}}\left(\mathbf{0},\sigma_{T}^{2}I\right)
$$  

2.3 FISHER INFORMATION IN DIFFUSION MODELS  

The Fisher information matrix in DMs is defined as the negative Hessian of the marginal log-density function, which takes the following matrix-valued form Song et al. (2021); Song $\&$ Lai (2024):  

$$
F_{t}(\mathbf{x}_{t},t):=-\frac{\partial^{2}}{\partial\mathbf{x}_{t}^{2}}\log q_{t}\left(\mathbf{x}_{t},t\right)
$$  

The current technique typically approximately accesses to the Fisher information by accessing the scaled Jacobian matrix of the learned score estimator network $\varepsilon_{\theta}$ :  

$$
\begin{array}{r l}&{F_{t}(x_{t},t)=-\frac{\partial}{\partial x_{t}}\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial x_{t}}\log p\left(x_{t},t\right)\right)}\\ &{\qquad\qquad\approx-\frac{\partial}{\partial x_{t}}\left(-\frac{\varepsilon_{\theta}\left(x_{t},t\right)}{\sigma_{t}}\right)=\frac{1}{\sigma_{t}}\frac{\partial\varepsilon_{\theta}\left(x_{t},t\right)}{\partial x_{t}}}\end{array}
$$  

The full Fisher information matrix within DMs cannot be obtained due to dimensional constraints. For instance, the Stable Diffusion-1.5 model (Rombach et al., 2022) features a latent dimension of $d\,=\,4\,\times\,64\,\times\,64\,=\,16384$ , resulting in a Fisher matrix of $16384\,\times\,16384$ . Fortunately, for applications that only need to access the trace or multiplication of Fisher information, it is feasible to use Jacobian-vector-product (JVP) to access Fisher information. For any $d$ -dimensional vector $\pmb{v}$ , the approximation of $\pmb{v}$ left multiplied by $\pmb{F}_{t}(\pmb{x}_{t},t)$ using JVP is as follows:  

$$
F_{t}(x_{t},t)v\approx\frac{1}{\sigma_{t}}\frac{\partial\varepsilon_{\theta}(x_{t},t)}{\partial x_{t}}v=\frac{1}{\sigma_{t}}\frac{\partial\left[\langle\varepsilon_{\theta}(x_{t},t)|v\rangle\right]}{\partial x_{t}}
$$  

The JVP is a time-consuming process due to its requirement for gradient calculations within the neural network. In addition, empirical evidence from synthetic distributions, as demonstrated in Lu et al. (2022a), shows that the approximation results from the JVP significantly deviate from the true underlying Fisher information. To our knowledge, there is no theoretical guarantee that Fisher information can be accurately accessed through the JVP. Moreover, the JVP fails to provide any theoretical insight into the Fisher information of diffused distributions.  



\section{3 ANALYTICAL FISHER INFORMATION  }


Accessing Fisher information via the JVP as shown in equation 9 is straightforward, but it does not take advantage of any inherent structure of the diffused distribution. In this section, we initially derive the analytical Fisher information (AFI) of diffused distribution under a simplified setting, where  

![](images/656ee183380c5476e43ae14424c26fc76d908b871fd681130669716e63c6a2af.jpg)  

Table 1: Comparison of Jacobian-vector-product (JVP) and our analytical-Fisher-information (AFI) in terms of per-iteration theoretical time-cost, practical time-cost, and approximation error bound. The theoretical time-cost is based on assumptions of a network access cost of $c_{1}d$ time and a backpropagation on network cost of $c_{2}d$ time $(c_{2}\,\approx\,4c_{1})$ ). The practical time-cost is tested using the SD-V1.5 model over $10\mathbf{k}$ COCO prompts.5.  

we assume that the initial $q_{0}$ is a sum of Dirac. Subsequently, we extend the AFI to a more general setting. Importantly, the AFI obtained in both settings does not involve any gradient calculations and is expressed into the initial data distribution, thus enabling the derivation of novel algorithms. Several studies Lu et al. (2022a); Benton et al. (2024) have investigated a similar form, but have not expressed it in terms of the initial data distribution. Our formulation can also be derived from a transformation of their formula.4.  

3.1 THE DIRAC SETTING  

We start with a simple setting where we assume that the initial distribution is characterized as a sum of Dirac distributions composed of the set of samples in the dataset. If we suppose the dataset is denoted as $\{y_{i}\}_{i=0}^{N}$ , then the initial distribution follows  

$$
\mathrm{(Dirac\;Setting)}\qquad q({\pmb x},t)|_{t=0}=\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=0}^{N}\delta({\pmb x}-{\pmb y}_{i}),
$$  

where exists a $0<\mathcal{D}_{y}<\infty$ such that $\|y_{i}\|\leq\mathcal{D}_{y}$ holds true for every $i$ . In this Dirac setting, we derive the following Analytical Fisher Information, which is a weighted outer-product sum devoid of gradients and composed solely of the initial distribution and the noise schedule.  

Proposition 1. Defines $v_{i}(\pmb{x}_{t},t)$ as $\begin{array}{r}{\exp\biggl(-\frac{|\pmb{x}_{t}-\alpha_{t}\pmb{y}_{i}|^{2}}{2\sigma_{t}^{2}}\biggr)\in\mathbb{R}}\end{array}$ and $\begin{array}{r}{w_{i}(\mathbf{x}_{t},t)\;a s\;\frac{v_{i}(\mathbf{x}_{t},t)}{\sum_{j}v_{j}(\mathbf{x}_{t},t)}\in}\end{array}$ $\mathbb{R}_{}$ . If $q_{0}$ takes the form as in equation equation $I O$ , the Fisher information matrix of the diffused distribution $q_{t}$ for $t\in(0,1]$ can be analytically formulated as follows:  

$$
F_{t}(\boldsymbol{x}_{t},t)=\frac{1}{\sigma_{t}^{2}}I-\frac{\alpha_{t}^{2}}{\sigma_{t}^{4}}\left[\sum_{i}w_{i}y_{i}y_{i}^{\top}-\left(\sum_{i}w_{i}y_{i}\right)\left(\sum_{i}w_{i}y_{i}\right)^{\top}\right]
$$  

where we have simplified $w_{i}(\pmb{x}_{t},t)$ to $w_{i}$ , as it does not lead to any confusion.  

We also find that the $\textstyle\sum_{i}w_{i}\pmb{y}_{i}$ component in equation 11 can be effectively approximated by the trained score network  in the form of $y$ -prediction, as demonstrated in the following proposition.  

Proposition 2. Given the diffusion training loss in equation 4, and if q0 conforms to the form presented in equation $I O$ , then the optimal $\bar{\pmb{y}}_{\theta}(\pmb{x}_{t},t)$ can accurately estimate $\sum_{i}w_{i}\pmb{y}_{i}$ .  



\section{3.2 THE GENERAL SETTING  }


We then begin to extend the AFI in equation 11 to a more general setting, where we only assume that the initial distribution $q_{0}$ is a measure on $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ with finite second momentum.  

$$
\begin{array}{r l}{(\mathrm{General~Setting})\quad}&{{}q_{0}\in\mathcal{P}_{2}(\mathbb{R}^{d}).}\end{array}
$$  

In this general setting, we derive the following Analytical Fisher Information, which is a weighted outer-product integral devoid of gradients.  

![](images/2e87f7fa8575d67f6349fbb04f2ea5a9aeef4b808cfbf3f23a353b4aab2de5bc.jpg)  

![](images/116c8d0b9e576767eda1fa3b236873198a6680a08112804e0070fd9c09e34bd0.jpg)  
Figure 1: The training loss of AFI-TM for SD- Figure 2: The trades-off curve of NLL and Clip 1.5 and SD-2base. It demonstrates commendable score of SD-1.5 and SD-2base across various convergence behavior. guidance scales in [1.5, 2.5, ..., 12.5, 13.5]  

Proposition 3. Let us define $v(\pmb{x}_{t},t,\pmb{y})$ as $\exp\left(-\frac{|\pmb{x}_{t}-\alpha_{t}\pmb{y}|^{2}}{2\sigma_{t}^{2}}\right)\;\in\;\mathbb{R}$ and $w(\pmb{x}_{t},t,\pmb{y})$ as $\begin{array}{r}{\frac{v\left(\pmb{x}_{t},t,\pmb{y}\right)}{\int_{\mathbb{R}^{d}}v\left(\pmb{x}_{t},t,\pmb{y}\right)\mathrm{d}q_{0}\left(\pmb{y}\right)}\in\mathbb{R}.}\end{array}$ If $q_{0}$ takes the form as in equation $^{12}$ , the Fisher information matrix of the diffused distribution $q_{t}$ for $t\in(0,1]$ can be analytically formulated as follows:  

$$
(G e n e r a l\,A F I)\;\;\;F_{t}(x_{t},t)\!=\!{\frac{1}{\sigma_{t}^{2}}}{\cal I}\!-\!{\frac{\alpha_{t}^{2}}{\sigma_{t}^{4}}}\left[\int w(y)y y^{\top}\mathrm{d}q_{0}\!-\!\left(\int w(y)y\mathrm{d}q_{0}\right)\left(\int w(y)y\mathrm{d}q_{0}\right)^{\top}\right]
$$  

We further ascertain that the $\begin{array}{r}{\int w(\pmb{y})y\mathrm{d}q_{0}(\pmb{y})}\end{array}$ component in equation 13 can be effectively approximated by the score network in the form of $y$ -prediction, as demonstrated in the following proposition.  

Proposition 4. Given the diffusion loss in equation 4, and if $q_{0}$ conforms to the form in equation 12, then the optimal $\bar{\pmb{y}}_{\theta}(\pmb{x}_{t},t)$ can accurately estimate $\begin{array}{r}{\int w(\pmb{y})y\mathrm{d}q_{0}(\pmb{y})}\end{array}$ .  

The derivation of the AFI under the general setting is akin to the sum in the Dirac setting but in an integral form. For the remainder of the paper, we will focus on developing our method based on the Dirac setting AFI. However, the same results can be naturally extended to the general setting.  



\section{4 AFI TRACE MATCHING (AFI-TM) METHOD  }


The likelihood evaluation of DMs would require access to Fisher Information’s trace. In this section, we introduce a network to learn the trace, thus facilitating effective likelihood evaluation in DMs.  

Log-Likelihood in DMs Log-likelihood is a classic and significant metric for probabilistic generative models, extensively utilized for comparison between samples or models Bengio et al. (2013); Theis et al. (2015). According to Chen et al. (2018); Song et al. (2021), The log-likelihood of samples generated by PF-ODE in equation 6 from DMs can be computed through a connection to continuous normalizing flows as follows:  

$$
{\begin{array}{r l}&{{\cfrac{\partial\log q_{t}(\mathbf{x}_{t},t)}{\partial t}}=-\mathrm{tr}\left({\cfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{t}}}\left(f(t)\mathbf{x}_{t}-{\cfrac{1}{2}}g^{2}(t)\partial_{x_{t}}\log q_{t}(\mathbf{x}_{t},t)\right)\right)}\\ &{\qquad\qquad=-\mathrm{tr}\left(\left(f(t)I-{\cfrac{1}{2}}g^{2}(t){\cfrac{\partial^{2}}{\partial x_{t}}}\log q_{t}(\mathbf{x}_{t},t)\right)\right)}\\ &{\qquad\qquad=-f(t)d-{\cfrac{g^{2}(t)}{2}}\mathrm{tr}\left(F_{t}(\mathbf{x}_{t},t)\right)}\end{array}}
$$  

where $\mathrm{{tr}}(\cdot)$ denotes the trace of a matrix, which is defined to be the sum of elements on the diagonal.  

Log-Likelihood Evaluation via JVP The current technique is only capable of conducting backpropagation of scalar value to the neural network. Therefore, the JVP in equation 9 cannot directly calculate the trace of the Fisher information. The JVP must iterate through each dimension to compute the individual elements on the diagonal, and then sum them up as follows  

$$
\left(\mathrm{JVP\;for\;trace}\right)\qquad\mathrm{tr}\left(F_{t}({x}_{t},t)\right)\approx\frac{1}{\sigma_{t}}\sum_{i=1}^{d}\frac{\partial\left[\left\langle\varepsilon_{\theta}({x}_{t},t)\middle|e^{(i)}\right\rangle\right]}{\partial x_{t}}.
$$  

Evaluating the trace using the JVP method would be extremely time-consuming due to the curse of dimensionality. If the time-complexity of a single backpropagation is $O(d)$ , then the calculation in equation 15 would have a time-complexity of $\bar{O}(d^{2})$ . In practice, as demonstrated in Table 1, evaluating the trace of Fisher information on the SD-1.5 model would require half an hour, rendering it nearly infeasible.  

Gradient-free Log-Likelihood Evaluation via AFI trace matching To overcome the limitations of the JVP method in evaluating the trace of the Fisher information, we propose to directly obtain its analytical form. Given the AFI in Proposition 1, we can also derive its trace in an analytical form of weighted norm sum, as highlighted in the following proposition:  

Proposition 5. In the same context as Proposition $^{\,I}$ , the trace of the Fisher information matrix for the diffused distribution $q_{t}$ , where $t\in(0,1],$ , is given by:  

$$
\operatorname{tr}\left(F_{t}(\pmb{x}_{t},t)\right)=\frac{d}{\sigma_{t}^{2}}-\frac{\alpha_{t}^{2}}{\sigma_{t}^{4}}\left[\sum_{i}w_{i}\|\pmb{y}_{i}\|^{2}-\left\|\sum_{i}w_{i}\pmb{y}_{i}\right\|^{2}\right]
$$  

As demonstrated in Proposition 2, the $\left\|\sum_{i}w_{i}\pmb{y}_{i}\right\|^{2}$ can be directly estimated by $\|\bar{\pmb{y}}_{\theta}(\pmb{x}_{t},t)\|^{2}$ . Therefore, the only unknown element in equation 16 is $\sum_{i}w_{i}\big|\big|\pmb{\{y}}_{i}\big|\big|^{2}$ . Consequently, we suggest estimating this term using a scalar-valued neural network, as per the following training algorithm:  



\section{5 AFI ENDPOINT APPROXIMATION (AFI-EA) METHOD  }


The adjoint optimization of DMs would require applying Fisher Information as a linear operator. In this section, we present a training-free method that simplifies the complex linear transformation calculations thus enabling faster and more accurate adjoint optimization.  

Adjoint optimization sampling. Guided sampling techniques are extensively utilized in diffusion models to facilitate controllable generation. Recently, to address the inflexibility of commonly used classifier-based guidance (Dhariwal & Nichol, 2021b) and classifier-free guidance (Ho & Salimans, 2022), a series of training-free adjoint guidance methods have been investigated and explored (Pan et al., 2023a;b).  

Consider optimizing a scalar-valued loss function $\mathcal{L}(\cdot):\mathbb{R}^{d}\mapsto\mathbb{R}$ , which takes $\scriptstyle x_{0}$ in the data space as input. Adjoint guidance is implemented by applying gradient descent on $\pmb{x}_{t}$ in the direction of ∂L(∂x0x(txt)). The essence of adjoint guidance is to use the gradient at t = 0 and follow the adjoint ODE (Pollini et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2018) to compute $\begin{array}{r}{\pmb{\lambda}_{t}:=\frac{\partial\mathcal{L}(\pmb{x}_{0}(\pmb{x}_{t}))}{\partial\pmb{x}_{t}}}\end{array}$ ∂L(x0(xt))for t > 0.  

$$
{\frac{\mathrm{d}\lambda_{t}}{\mathrm{d}t}}=-{\frac{\partial h_{\theta}\left(x_{t},t\right)}{\partial x_{t}}}^{\top}\lambda_{t},\quad\lambda_{0}={\frac{\partial{\mathcal{L}}(x_{0})}{\partial x_{0}}}
$$  

Adjoint ODE via JVP. Regardless of the ODE solver being used, it is necessary to compute the right-hand-side of equation 18, or equivalently, $\pmb{F}(\pmb{x}_{t},t)^{\top}\pmb{\lambda}_{t}$ . This computation can be interpreted as applying the Fisher information matrix as a linear operator to the adjoint state $\lambda_{t}$ , from a functional analysis perspective (Yosida, 2012). Current practices utilize the JVP technique to approximate this linear transformation operation as follows:  

$$
{\mathrm{~/~for~Adjoint)}}\qquad\quad F({x_{t},t})^{\top}\lambda_{t}\approx{\frac{1}{\sigma_{t}}}{\frac{\partial\varepsilon_{\theta}\left(x_{t},t\right)^{\top}}{\partial x_{t}}}^{\top}\lambda_{t}\approx{\frac{1}{\sigma_{t}}}{\frac{\partial\left[\langle\varepsilon_{\theta}\left(x_{t},t\right)|\lambda_{t}\rangle\right]}{\partial x_{t}}}
$$  

![](images/9def4ac0ff208eecb9d732d2ab025b261698b3995f7b79d8b5bfc7c417ccd14c.jpg)  
Figure 5: Qualitative comparison of AFI-EA (Ours) and JVP in the adjoint aesthetic improvement task. AFI-EA consistently generates high-aesthetic images with reduced time expenditure. The smoother visual effect is a desirable outcome of the target score, but not a result of our AFI-EA.  

This process involves computationally intensive gradient operations on the neural network, and the approximation errors introduced by the JVP technique have no theoretical bound.  

Adjoint ODE via AFI-EA. As previously discussed in Section 3, the AFI inherently doesn’t require gradients, suggesting that we could potentially apply the Fisher information as a linear operator in a gradient-free manner. The challenging part in equation 11 is $\textstyle\sum_{i}w_{i}{\pmb y}_{i}{\pmb y}_{i}^{\top}$ , which represents a weighted form of outer-products of data. Based on the definition of $w_{i}$ , the closest $\pmb{y}_{i}$ to $\pmb{x}_{0}$ will dominate as $t\,\rightarrow\,0$ . This makes it intuitive to replace this sum with a single final sample outerproduct $\mathbf{\Delta}x_{0}x_{0}^{\top}$ . It’s also important to note that the adjoint guidance itself needs to compute $\pmb{x}_{0}$ at each guidance step, eliminating the need for additional computation to obtain $\pmb{x}_{0}$ . Given that we utilize the endpoint sample $\pmb{x}_{0}$ , we refer to this approximation technique as AFI Endpoint Approximation (EA). The formulation for AFI-EA in adjoint ODE is as follows:  

$$
\begin{array}{r l}&{\boldsymbol{F}(\boldsymbol{x}_{t},t)^{\top}\lambda_{t}\approx\left(\frac{1}{\sigma_{t}^{2}}\boldsymbol{I}-\frac{\alpha_{t}^{2}}{\sigma_{t}^{4}}\left(\sum_{i}w_{i}y_{i}y_{i}^{\top}-\bar{y}_{\theta}(\boldsymbol{x}_{t},t)\bar{y}_{\theta}(\boldsymbol{x}_{t},t)^{\top}\right)\right)^{\top}\lambda_{t}}\\ &{\qquad\qquad\approx\left(\frac{1}{\sigma_{t}^{2}}\boldsymbol{I}-\frac{\alpha_{t}^{2}}{\sigma_{t}^{4}}\left(\boldsymbol{x}_{0}\boldsymbol{x}_{0}^{\top}-\bar{y}_{\theta}(\boldsymbol{x}_{t},t)\bar{y}_{\theta}(\boldsymbol{x}_{t},t)^{\top}\right)\right)^{\top}\lambda_{t}}\\ &{\qquad\qquad=\frac{1}{\sigma_{t}^{2}}\lambda_{t}-\frac{\alpha_{t}^{2}}{\sigma_{t}^{4}}\left(\boldsymbol{x}_{0},\lambda_{t}\right)\boldsymbol{x}_{0}+\frac{\alpha_{t}^{2}}{\sigma_{t}^{4}}\left<\bar{y}_{\theta}(\boldsymbol{x}_{t},t),\lambda_{t}\right>\bar{y}_{\theta}(\boldsymbol{x}_{t},t)}\end{array}
$$  

The AFI-EA approximation leads to a scalar-weighted combination of $\lambda_{t},x_{0}$ , and $\bar{\pmb{y}}_{\theta}(\pmb{x}_{t},t)$ , which importantly, does not involve any gradients. Additionally, we derive the theoretical approximation error bound of the AFI-EA in Proposition 8. To measure the accuracy of AFI-EA as a linear operator, we opt to use the Hilbert–Schmidt norm (Gohberg et al., 1990) for measurement, as follows:  

Proposition 8. Assume that the approximation error on $\pmb{\varepsilon}_{\theta}(\pmb{x}_{t},t)$ is denoted as $\delta_{2}$ , the approximation error of the endpoint approximated Fisher linear operator, as referenced in 20, is at most  t3 $\frac{\alpha_{t}^{2}}{\sigma_{t}^{3}}\left(2\mathcal{D}_{y}^{2}+\sqrt{d}\delta_{2}\right)$ when measured in terms of the Hilbert–Schmidt norm.  

Experiments on AFI-EA. As depicted in Figure 4, we conducted experiments comparing our AFI-EA and JVP methods in adjoint guidance sampling, using four different scores and two different base models. AFI-EA consistently achieves better scores due to its bounded approximation error. Furthermore, AFI-EA requires less processing time as it eliminates the need for time-consuming gradient operations. AFI-EA and JVP are compared under the same guidance scales and schemes across various numbers of steps.2.  

As depicted in Figure 5, our AFI-EA consistently generates samples with higher aesthetic scores with a reduced time-cost compared to JVP. It’s worth noting that this enhancement in aesthetics results in final images that are more vibrant and smoother.2.  



\section{6 THEOREM ON THE OT PROPERTY OF THE PF-ODE DEDUCED MAP  }


There is an increasing trend towards analyzing the probability modeling capabilities of DMs by interpreting them from an optimal transport perspective (Albergo et al., 2023; Chen et al., 2024).9. One of the central questions is whether the map deduced by the PF-ODE could represent an optimal transport. If so, how should we design the noise schedule, or what conditions should the data distribution meet? For a specific noise schedule where $f(t)\equiv0$ , Zhang et al. (2024a) demonstrates that having all data points lying on a single line is a sufficient condition for the map to represent an optimal transport.  

In this section, we find out that the AFI, as derived in section 3, can contribute to the first equivalence condition for the OT property of PF-ODE deduced mapping under a general noise schedule. We refer to this theorem as the AFI Optimal Transport (AFI-OT) theorem. The AFI-OT condition is presented in the following:  

Theorem 1. Denote the diffeomorphism deduced by the PF-ODE 5 as follows  

$$
T_{s,t}:\mathbb{R}^{n}\longrightarrow\mathbb{R}^{n};\mathbf{x}_{s}\longmapsto x_{t},\quad\forall t\geq s>0.
$$  

This diffeomorphism is well-posed guaranteed by the global version of Picard-Lindel¨of theorem Amann (2011); Zhang et al. (2024a). The diffeomorphism $T_{s,T}$ is a Monge optimal transport map if and only if the normalized fundamental matrix for $B(t)\equiv B(t,{\pmb x}_{t})$ at $\boldsymbol{s}$ is semi-positive definite for every $P F$ -ODE chain start from a $\pmb{x}_{T}\in\mathbb{R}^{d}$ . where  

$$
B(t,x_{t})=\left[f(t)-\frac{g^{2}(t)}{2\sigma_{t}^{2}}\right]I+\frac{\alpha_{t}^{2}g^{2}(t)}{2\sigma_{t}^{4}}\left[\sum_{i}w_{i}y_{i}y_{i}^{\top}-\left(\sum_{i}w_{i}y_{i}\right)\left(\sum_{i}w_{i}y_{i}\right)^{\top}\right].
$$  

The outline of the proof is as follows: We first apply Brenier’s theorem (Brenier, 1991; Santambrogio, 2015) to convert the problem of whether the PF-ODE mapping is an optimal transport into the task of finding a convex potential, where the existence is guaranteed by the Poincare´’s Theorem (Lang, 2012). We then use adjoint methods to express the second derivatives of the convex potential function in the form of an integral of information Rockafellar (2015); Pollini et al. (2018). Finally, we apply matrix exponential integration theory Masuyama (2016) to reformulate the condition into the AFI-OT theorem.10.  



\section{7 CONCLUSIONS  }


This paper introduced the Analytical Fisher Information (AFI), an analytical formulation that allows for more efficient and theoretical exploration of Fisher Information of diffused distribution. Practically, we have proposed two algorithmic variants of AFI for different scenarios: AFI trace matching (AFI-TM) and AFI endpoint approximation (AFI-EA). Both methods are gradient-free, theoretically guaranteed for approximation error bounds and convergence properties, and offer improved accuracy and reduced time-cost compared to the traditional JVP method. Theoretically, we have established the first general theorem for the PF-ODE map to be optimal transport. This work not only improves the efficiency of Fisher Information evaluation but also widens our understanding of the diffused distributions.

content.summary: This paper proposed a way to approximate the Jacobian of the score function in a diffusion model without taking gradients. Based on expressing the Jacobian by a conditional covariance, the paper proposed some methods to estimate the Jacobian, the trace of the Jacobian,  and matrix-vector products with Jacobian. In the end of the paper, a criterion of whether the probability flow is an optimal transport map was also claimed.
content.strengths: This paper is clearly written. Core ideas are presented in a very readable manner, and there are no unnecessary complications.
content.weaknesses:
While the clarity of writing in this paper is greatly appreciated, I regret to say that this paper has some major issues that resist a more positive evaluation.

1. The key result, Proposition 3 in the paper, is well-known. Most of the theory papers on diffusion contained a version of it, e.g., Benton et al., ICLR 2024 had exactly the same result.
